coredata.dc:title: Turning built-in electric field of porphyrin on Ti3+ self-doped blue-TiO2 hollow nanospheres boosts peroxidase-like activity for high-performance biosensing
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Qi
Sun, Qiqi
Gao, Wen
Shen, Jingshun
Zhang, Yuming
Lu, Guang
Chen, Yanli
Yu, Sirong
Li, Xiyou
coredata.dc:description: The four well-designed porphyrins are synthesized with different electronegativity groups at the meso-substituted phenyl groups to turn the built-in electric field of the porphyrins. Among them, the meso-tetrakis(meta-hydroxyphenyl) porphyrin (mTHPP) with the largest diploes is revealed to form the strongest built-in electric field on the surface of Ti3+ self-doped blue TiO2 hollow nanospheres (b-TiO2). The b-TiO2 with Fenton-like activity couple mTHPP with a mass ration (the mass of mTHPP to the total sample mass) of 29% by stable hydrogen and electrostatic bonding, resulting in b-TiO2/29mTHPP. A synergistic effect of strong built-in electric field and the well-matching energy levels between mTHPP and b-TiO2 promotes the rapid multi-channel transmission of photo-generated electrons from mTHPP to b-TiO2. Consequently, the outstanding peroxidase (POD) -like activity achieved for b-TiO2/29mTHPP with the fast oxidation of TMB (3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine) by H2O2 to form the blue product (oxTMB) only in 10 s under visible light. Furthermore, the b-TiO2/29mTHPP combined with cholesterol oxidase (Chox) can quantitatively determine cholesterol in the range of 0.005–10 mM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 1.2 μM within 1 min. The present sensing performance represents one of the best results for the cholesterol colorimetric sensor reported so far. Furthermore, this colorimetric biosensor has been used for accurately determining the levels of total cholesterol in serum samples, indicating the great potential in clinical medical detection. The present work provides new perspectives to design various POD-like nanozyme for biosensing applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Chemical structures of the porphyrins 5,10,15,20-tetrakis(phenyl) porphyrin (TPP) (A), pTCPP (B), 5,10,15,20-tetrakis(4-(hydroxyl) phenyl) porphyrin (pTHPP) (C) and mTHPP (D); The schematic diagram of the preparation of the hollow nanosphere of Ti3+ self-doped b-TiO2/29mTHPP nanozyme (E).

SEM of SiO2 nanospheres (A), SiO2/TiO2 nanospheres (B), b-TiO2 hollow nanospheres (C), b-TiO2/29mTHPP (E), TEM of b-TiO2 hollow nanospheres (D) (Insert: HRTEM of b-TiO2 hollow nanozymes), b-TiO2/29mTHPP (F), HADDF-TEM and EDS mapping images of b-TiO2/29mTHPP hollow nanozymes (G) (C red, N yellow, O green, Ti blue).

The XRD of mTHPP, TiO2, b-TiO2 and b-TiO2/29mTHPP (A), UV–vis DRS of the prepared samples (B), ESR spectra detecting oxygen vacancy of TiO2, b-TiO2 and b-TiO2/29mTHPP (C), The Raman spectrum of b-TiO2/29mTHPP, TiO2/29mTHPP, b-TiO2, TiO2 and mTHPP in 50–3000 cm−1 (D), 50–800 cm−1 (E), 1000–2000 cm−1 (F).
The XPS of the b-TiO2/29mTHPP nanozyme: Ti 2p spectra (A), O 1 s spectra (B), C 1 s spectra (C), N 1 s spectra (D).

UV–vis of b-TiO2/29mTHPP(a), b-TiO2/29pTHPP(b), b-TiO2/29pTCPP(c), TiO2/29mTHPP(d), b-TiO2/29TPP(e), b-TiO2(f), TiO2(g), mTHPP (h) in the H2O2/TMB solution, b-TiO2/29mTHPP without H2O2 (i) H2O2 + TMB(j), and TMB(k) under light, corresponding photograph (inset) (A); UV–vis DRS of different samples (B); Comparisons of band structure of b-TiO2 and four porphyrins (C); PL spectrum of mTHPP, TiO2/29mTHPP and b-TiO2/29mTHPP (D); Transient photocurrent response (E) and EIS Nyquist plots (F) of b-TiO2/29mTHPP (a), TiO2/29mTHPP (b), b-TiO2 (c), mTHPP (d) and TiO2 (e).

The electrons distribution and molecular dipoles in four porphyrins, [The direction of the arrow is the direction of the built-in electric field] (A); Diagram illustration of charge transfer for b-TiO2/29mTHPP (left) and b-TiO2/29pTCPP (right) under light (B).

UV–vis spectra of b-TiO2/29mTHPP (a), b-TiO2/29pTHPP (b), b-TiO2/29pTCPP (c), TiO2/29mTHPP (d), b-TiO2/29TPP (e), b-TiO2(f), TiO2 (g), mTHPP (h) in the H2O2/TMB solution, b-TiO2/29mTHPP without H2O2 (i) H2O2 + TMB(j), and TMB(k) under dark (A), Effects of various active scavengers on the absorbance intensity in “TMB + H2O2 + b-TiO2/mTHPP” system (B) and (C), Fluorescence intensity of TA + H2O2 + b-TiO2/mTHPP system at 430 nm varies with the amount of b-TiO2/mTHPP (D), Diagram illustration of b-TiO2/29mTHPP as a POD mimic for H2O2 (E).

UV–vis spectra of b-TiO2/29mTHPP in different cholesterol concentrations (A); Dose-response curve for cholesterol determination at 652 nm, the inset corresponds to the linear fitting curve for cholesterol (0.005–1.45 mM and 1.45–10 mM) (B); The selectivity of the b-TiO2/29mTHPP towards cholesterol (0.1 mM) and interferents (1 mM) (C); The competition experiment of cholesterol with the coexisting substances were detected in a solution containing cholesterol and one of the disrupters (D).

Results of total cholesterol determination in human serum samples. Note: Relative deviation = (A-A0)/A0 × 100 %, where A0 stands for the value of the medical examination report, and A stands for the value determined by the proposed approach.

head.ce:title: Turning built-in electric field of porphyrin on Ti3+ self-doped blue-TiO2 hollow nanospheres boosts peroxidase-like activity for high-performance biosensing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The four well-designed porphyrins are synthesized with different electronegativity groups at the meso-substituted phenyl groups to turn the built-in electric field of the porphyrins. Among them, the meso-tetrakis(meta-hydroxyphenyl) porphyrin (mTHPP) with the largest diploes is revealed to form the strongest built-in electric field on the surface of Ti3+ self-doped blue TiO2 hollow nanospheres (b-TiO2). The b-TiO2 with Fenton-like activity couple mTHPP with a mass ration (the mass of mTHPP to the total sample mass) of 29% by stable hydrogen and electrostatic bonding, resulting in b-TiO2/29mTHPP. A synergistic effect of strong built-in electric field and the well-matching energy levels between mTHPP and b-TiO2 promotes the rapid multi-channel transmission of photo-generated electrons from mTHPP to b-TiO2. Consequently, the outstanding peroxidase (POD) -like activity achieved for b-TiO2/29mTHPP with the fast oxidation of TMB (3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine) by H2O2 to form the blue product (oxTMB) only in 10 s under visible light. Furthermore, the b-TiO2/29mTHPP combined with cholesterol oxidase (Chox) can quantitatively determine cholesterol in the range of 0.005–10 mM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 1.2 μM within 1 min. The present sensing performance represents one of the best results for the cholesterol colorimetric sensor reported so far. Furthermore, this colorimetric biosensor has been used for accurately determining the levels of total cholesterol in serum samples, indicating the great potential in clinical medical detection. The present work provides new perspectives to design various POD-like nanozyme for biosensing applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the first nano- peroxidase (POD) mimic (Fe3O4 nanoparticles) was found and used for colorimetric sensing, various nanomaterials have been synthesized and studied for POD-like catalytic activity, such as precious metal nanoclusters [1], nano-oxides [2,3], and sulfides [4,5]. However, the catalytic activity of the single component POD mimics usually maintains a relatively poor level that takes a long time to complete the colorimetric reaction. Therefore, the nanocomposites that combined unique properties offered by muti-components on a nanoscale are expected to achieve the improvement of overall catalytic activity, such as Si-CoO [6], PdFe/GDY [7], Co3O4@Co-Fe [8], and so on [9,10].

On the other hand, for some inorganic semiconductors, it has been found that the introduction of suitable photosensitizers to construct heterojunctions can effectively limit the recombination of photogenerated carriers and thus significantly increase the POD-like activity under visible light [11]. Porphyrins and their derivatives, as a typical representative of macrocyclic molecular materials with strong colors, exhibit high absorbance in the visible and near-infrared regions, which is necessary for efficient photon harvesting [12]. Furthermore, the properties such as great processability, rich substituents, excellent redox performance, and good chemical stability make them the best candidates for the photosensitizers [13,14]. For instance, by using 5,10,15,20-tetrakis (4-carboxylphenyl) porphyrin (pTCPP) binding to different inorganic nanoparticles (ZnS, Co3O4, NiO, PCN-224 and etc.) POD-like catalysts are obtained with different activities [15–19]. Although there have been some excellent works in this field, most efforts have been focused on various inorganic nanoparticles functionalized by one porphyrin derivative. The studies on POD-like activity of Por-based organic–inorganic nanozymes by the reasonable selection of porphyrin photosensitizers with suitable functional substituents on the periphery of the porphyrin core have not been explored yet.

Another challenge encountered in producing high-quality POD-like catalysts is to prevent nanoparticle agglomeration and facilitate more active site exposure through morphological control [20]. For photoactive materials, the hollow nanosphere would be a commendable choice because the hollow structure could maintain multiple scattering to increase visible light absorption. Furthermore, the inner-outer walls of the hollow nanospheres are expected to provide double specific surface areas to increase the reaction sites [21]. By combining the advantages of hollow nanospheres and reasonable porphyrins, enhanced POD-like activity of the artificial enzymes is highly expected.

With this background in mind, herein, we report the preparation and characterization of the Ti3+ self-doped blue TiO2 hollow nanospheres (b-TiO2) sensitized with four metal-free meso-substituted porphyrins in Fig. 1 . Density functional theory (DFT) calculation results reveal that 5,10,15,20-tetrakis(meta-hydroxyphenyl) porphyrin (mTHPP) presents the largest diploes among these derivatives, thereby creating a significant difference in potential between ring center and terminal substituents. This benefits the effective separation of photogenerated carriers on mTHPP. In addition, due to the contribution of the electron-donating substituents to the energy levels of the porphyrins, the energy levels shift toward the positive direction, thereby better matching the energy level of b-TiO2 and promoting charge transfer between them. Therefore, the largest built-in electric field with proper direction formed in mTHPP is conducive to the rapid multi-channel transmission of photogenerated electrons from mTHPP to b-TiO2. When evaluated as POD-like nanozyme by the oxidation of TMB chromogenic substrate by H2O2, the b-TiO2/29mTHPP hollow nanospheres exhibit outstanding performances with a fast color change only in 10 s. The superior POD-like activity of b-TiO2/29mTHPP was combined with cholesterol oxidase (Chox) to construct a rapid colorimetric sensing platform for the determination of total cholesterol in serum samples.

All chemical reagents are of analytical grade purity and used directly without further purification. TPP, pTCPP, pTHPP, and mTHPP were synthesized and characterized by MALDI-TOF mass spectrum (Fig. S1), UV–vis spectra (Fig. S2), and elemental analysis as detailed in the section of synthesis of porphyrin derivatives (Supplementary Material).

As shown in Fig. 1E, the b-TiO2/29mTHPP hollow nanozyme were prepared via hydrothermal synthesis using SiO2 nanospheres as a template, then sculpturing SiO2 by the NH4HF2 and reducing TiO2 (TiO2 hollow spheres) by NaBH4 to obtain Ti3+ self-doped blue TiO2 hollow nanospheres, finally loading mTHPP in an optimal mass ratio of 29 wt% (the details in Fig. S3) on the hollow nanospheres by an ultrasound impregnation method, as detailed in the experimental section (Supplementary Material).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of b-TiO2/29mTHPP nanozyme
ce:section.ce:para: In the first place, we tried to couple the mTHPP with b-TiO2 via noncovalent bonding. The b-TiO2 hollow nanospheres were prepared by using the continuous hydrothermal and sculpture-reduction processes, where surface hydroxyl and oxygen vacancy (Ov) could be generated [22]. In this way, more mTHPP can be anchored to b-TiO2 hollow nanospheres to form a relatively stable b-TiO2/29mTHPP composite [23].

As shown in Fig. 2 A, the smooth and uniform SiO2 nanospheres are successfully obtained with diameter of about 300 nm. After the deposition of the TiO2 on SiO2 nanosphere template (Fig. 2B), the smooth surface of the ball becomes rough with a slightly larger diameter than that of SiO2 nanosphere, implying the formation of a dense TiO2 shell on the surface of the SiO2 nanosphere. Further, upon the sculpture and reduction, the hollow b-TiO2 shell has formed due to the dissolution of SiO2 nanosphere template in Fig. 2C. Carefully, the hollow shells are composed of numerous small nanoparticles, showing a rough and porous surface, which provide abundant active sites for catalytic reactions. The thickness of the hollow shell of b-TiO2 nanosphere is about 15 nm with diameter of about 300 nm in Fig. 2D. Additionally, the HRTEM of b-TiO2 exhibited the lattice space of 0.350 nm, which is ascribed to the (101) plane of the b-TiO2 (insert of Fig. 2D) [24], After further loading of mTHPP, as displayed in Fig. 2E and F, the size and morphology of the hollow shell did not change significantly, indicating that the introduction of mTHPP little affects the morphology. As we know, the unique hollow structures are beneficial for heterogeneous catalytic reactions. The light scattering and reflection effects caused by the hollow structure can enhance the absorption and utilization of visible light [21]. Moreover, the thin-shelled topologies would reduce the charge transport distance and thus promote efficient carrier transport [25]. All these features are desirable for promoting the catalytic performance. The elemental mapping image (Fig. 2G) shows the homogenous distribution of C, N, O and Ti elements. It can also be clearly seen that the distribution thickness of the C, N and O elements is obviously greater than that of Ti element, which indicates that the mTHPP are uniformly loaded on the inner and outer sides of the b-TiO2 hollow spherical shell.

XRD analysis was conducted to study the crystallinity of TiO2, b-TiO2 and b-TiO2/29mTHPP. All the samples were composed of anatase phases in Fig. 3 A [22]. The similarity of peaks of the three samples containing TiO2 or b-TiO2 suggests that the reduction and modification have maintained the crystallinity of TiO2, indicating a stable structure of the TiO2, which is beneficial for the continuous catalytic reaction [26]. And the sharp diffraction peaks of mTHPP are absent in b-TiO2/29mTHPP, indicating the destruction of long-range periodicity on the surface of b-TiO2 for mTHPP. Moreover, the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area of the b-TiO2/29mTHPP nanozymes is calculated through N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms. As shown in Fig. S4, compared with b-TiO2 hollow shell (116.3 m2/g), b-TiO2/29mTHPP possessed a slightly decreased specific surface area of 103.8 m2/g, which might be due to the filling of adsorption sites on the surface of the b-TiO2 hollow nanospheres by mTHPP molecules. Therefore, a suitable loading amount of photosensitizer on b-TiO2 is highly desired for enhancement of the catalytic activity of b-TiO2/29mTHPP nanozyme. In this case, an optimized loading amount of 29 wt% for mTHPP in b-TiO2/29mTHPP is obtained as mentioned above.

As shown in UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) (Fig. 3B and Table S1), compared to pure mTHPP in the solid state, the Soret band and Q bands of mTHPP in b-TiO2/29mTHPP nanozyme are blue-shifted and red-shifted, respectively. The changes in aggregation behavior for mTHPP in the b-TiO2/29mTHPP indicated that the anchoring effect of b-TiO2 on mTHPP reduced the intermolecular interaction of mTHPP [27,28]. Moreover, both Soret and Q bands of mTHPP in b-TiO2/29mTHPP nanozyme are all red-shifted and broadened compared to mTHPP in solution (Fig. 3B, S2 and Table S1), implying that there is still a significant field effect between mTHPP, which facilitates the formation of the built-in electric field [29]. Furthermore, as illustrated in Fig. 3B, the DRS of b-TiO2 exhibited an enhanced absorption in visible light compared to TiO2, which was attributed to the Ti3+ and oxygen vacancy (Ov) [22,30,31]. The Ov of samples formed in the reduction process was detected by electron spin resonance (ESR) measurements, as shown in Fig. 3C. No ESR signal was detected in the TiO2, whereas strong Ov signals were detected in b-TiO2 and b-TiO2/29mTHPP [31]. And the Ov intensities decreased with the introduction of mTHPP, which indicated that the presence of Ov enhances the anchoring ability of b-TiO2 to mTHPP, so the absorbance of b-TiO2/29mTHPP is much higher than that of TiO2/29mTHPP.

Raman and FT-IR spectroscopy are usually employed as powerful tool to determine the molecular structures and functional groups. The vibrational frequencies of the major peaks observed for white TiO2 are 143, 195, 394, 515, and 634 cm−1 as marked in Fig. 3D–E and Fig. S5, which are assigned to anatase TiO2 Raman active Eg mode (143 and 195 cm−1), B1g mode (394 cm−1), A1g mode (515 cm−1) and Eg mode (634 cm−1), respectively [32]. It’s interesting that, compared to those of TiO2, all the vibration peaks of b-TiO2 broaden and move slightly to the higher wavenumbers with the reduction, which is attributed to the presence of amorphous TiO2 in the b-TiO2 and also indicated the successful preparation of the b-TiO2 [33]. After further loading of mTHPP, the vibration peaks of TiO2 and b-TiO2 hardly changed significantly, indicating that the incorporation of mTHPP into the hollow spherical shell of the b-TiO2 did not change the lattice structure of TiO2 and b-TiO2. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 3F, the new spectroscopic features (1234, 1331, 1558, 1598 cm−1) from mTHPP in TiO2/29mTHPP and b-TiO2/29mTHPP can be detected, indicating that mTHPP was successfully introduced onto the surface of TiO2 and b-TiO2. Note that the characteristic peak of mTHPP in b-TiO2/29mTHPP is more obvious with the higher relative intensity than that in TiO2/29mTHPP (Fig. 3F). It should be attributed to a large number of Ov generated in the b-TiO2 lattice during the reduction process, which provides more active sites for mTHPP anchoring.

As shown in Fig. S6, the characteristic peaks at 1637 cm−1 and 550 cm−1 for the samples containing TiO2 and b-TiO2 were attributed to the –OH or physically absorbed water on the surface and Ti-O-Ti stretching vibrations [26,34]. Peaks at 1472 and 1221 cm−1 were attributed to O–H and C–O deformation vibration and stretching vibrations on phenolic hydroxyl group of mTHPP, respectively [35,36]. The relative intensity of the two peaks for mTHPP was significantly reduced and moved to the high wavenumber after being introduced into TiO2 and b-TiO2, which could be attributed to the hydrogen bond and electrostatic interaction between TiO2/ b-TiO2 and mTHPP. The vibrations of macrocycles in mTHPP donated the other three peaks at 1577 (C=C stretching vibration), 1435 (C–N stretching vibration), and 921 cm−1 (C–N bending vibration). However, the three peaks of macrocycles in mTHPP moved to the high wavenumber in TiO2/29mTHPP and b-TiO2/29mTHPP, which further showed the anchoring effect of TiO2 or b-TiO2 on mTHPP reduced the π-π intermolecular force of mTHPP and was consistent with the UV–vis results. In addition, compared with TiO2/29mTHPP, the peaks shape of b-TiO2/29mTHPP are more obvious and the peaks intensity are also higher, which are consistent with Raman spectra results. From the above, the binding modes include H-band, electrostatic and Ov binding between b-TiO2 and mTHPP are exhibited in Fig. S7.

The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) are carried out to elucidate the components and chemical states of the prepared b-TiO2/29mTHPP nanozyme. Fig. S8 presents the full survey, all the elements (Ti, O, C and N) of b-TiO2/29mTHPP were observed. The high-resolution XPS spectra of Ti 2p in b-TiO2/29mTHPP (Fig. 4 A) revealed a small peak at 457.7 eV in the deconvolution of the Ti 2p3/2 peak, which is associated with Ti ions in a reduced charge state (Ti3+) on the b-TiO2 [37]. In addition, the peaks at 458.9 and 464.5 eV are ascribed to the Ti4+ 2p3/2 and Ti4+ 2p1/2, respectively. Further, the three peaks at 529.7, 530.6, and 532.6 eV of the O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4B) could be assigned to the Ti-O bond from b-TiO2, the –OH adsorbed on the surface and meso- sites of mTHPP [34,38], and the oxygen vacancy (Ov) near Ti3+ [32], respectively. As revealed in Fig. 4C, the three distinct peaks at 284.7, 286.7 and 288.4 eV of C 1 s spectrum could be assigned to the C–C/C=C bonds, C–N bonds, and C–O bonds of mTHPP, respectively [39]. Lastly, as illustrated in Fig. 4D, the N 1 s spectrum could be only divided into one peak at 399.6 eV that is assigned to the pyrrole N. The XPS measurement further demonstrated the successful preparation of b-TiO2, and also indicated that mTHPP was successfully loaded on the surface of b-TiO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: POD-like activity and catalytic mechanism of b-TiO2/29mTHPP

ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the POD-like activity of b-TiO2/29mTHPP, eleven controlled experiments were designed and conducted in sodium acetate buffer of pH = 4 at 35 °C with 1 min under visible light (experimental optimization see Fig. S9A and S9B in Supplementary Material). As shown in Fig. 5 A and B, the system b-TiO2/29mTHPP + TMB + H2O2 (a) exhibited the strongest absorbance of all systems, accompanied by the rapid dark blue change (Only 10 s), which indicated that b-TiO2/29mTHPP possess strongly POD-like activity. The time-dependent absorbance changes at 652 nm for b-TiO2/29mTHPP + TMB + H2O2 system were also studied in Fig. S10. In fact, the absorbance at 652 nm gradually increased with the progress of the reaction, and its growth rate decreased significantly after 60 s until the equilibrium state appeared at 120 s in Fig. S10. And the POD-like activity of mTHPP, THPP, TCPP, and TPP modified b-TiO2 decreased in turn, Fig. 5A (a–c, and e). In addition, the absorbance of the system b-TiO2/29mTHPP + TMB and TMB + H2O2 (i and j) is very weak, indicating that catalysts b-TiO2/29mTHPP and H2O2 are necessary for the catalytic oxidation of TMB. A more noticeable improvement on POD-like activity of b-TiO2/29mTHPP than TiO2/29mTHPP in Fig. 5A suggested the b-TiO2 with Ov and self-doped Ti3+ has a more positive contribution to the performance than TiO2 [31]. Meanwhile three single component samples (f-h) all indicated poor catalytic activity in spite of a higher activity of b-TiO2 relative to that of TiO2 and mTHPP in Fig. 5A. The enhanced catalytic activity of b-TiO2 was mainly attributed to the built-in electric field of b-TiO2 induced by the TiO2/b-TiO2 (Ti3+/Ti4+) heterojunction [31], which effectively promoted the separation and transport of charge carriers, thereby improving the catalytic activity. As a result, the combined unique properties offered by both mTHPP and b-TiO2 components on a hollow nanosphere make such nanozyme the ultrahigh POD-like activity, which is attractive for the development and application in POD-like catalysts. To further compare the POD activity of different artificial nanozyme, the response time of colorimetric methods is displayed in Table S2. Obviously, the response time of proposed colorimetric methods based on b-TiO2/29mTHPP is much shorter than others, indicating that b-TiO2/29mTHPP possesses superior catalytic activity. This also indicated that the introduction of mTHPP as a photosensitizer effectively promoted the separation of carriers and accelerated the progress of the POD reaction. Therefore, b-TiO2/29mTHPP is considered an excellent candidate for designing sensors for real-time monitoring.

To understand the improved POD-like activity resulting from the self-doped Ti3+ in TiO2 and photosensitizer of mTHPP, the catalytic properties of b-TiO2/29mTHPP, TiO2/29mTHPP, b-TiO2/29TPP, b-TiO2/29pTCPP, b-TiO2/29pTHPP, and b-TiO2 were investigated using steady-state kinetics toward TMB and H2O2, respectively, in Fig. S11. The Michaelis–Menten constant Km and the maximal reaction velocity Vmax of the resulting samples were obtained using Lineweaver-Burk plots in Table S3. The Km value of b-TiO2/29mTHPP for TMB and H2O2 were 0.011 and 0.17 mM, respectively. Compared with the Km value (0.04 and 0.37 mM) of TiO2/29mTHPP for TMB and H2O2, these kinds of obvious decreases illustrated that Ov on b-TiO2 increases the affinity and activation for TMB and H2O2 [40]. Besides, when mTHPP was introduced into b-TiO2 hollow nanospheres, b-TiO2/29mTHPP exhibited the highest Vmax value for TMB (37.07 × 10−8 M s−1) and H2O2 (29.68 × 10−8 M s−1), compared to that of other nanozymes. Therefore, the introduction of mTHPP accelerated the POD-like reaction. Impressively, to the best of our knowledge, the POD-like activity of b-TiO2/29mTHPP in terms of both Km and Vmax represents one of the best performances achieved so far for mimic enzymes in Table S3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The thermodynamic for highly efficient charge separation of b-TiO2/29mTHPP
ce:section.ce:para: As we all know, the first step of photocatalysis is photon absorption. Increasing the light absorption of the material can greatly improve energy conversion efficiency. In consequence, it is essential to develop photocatalysts that can enhance the absorption and utilization of visible light. As shown in Fig. 5B, the UV–vis DRS of TPP, pTCPP, pTHPP, and mTHPP modified b-TiO2 hollow spheres showed the combined features of the b-TiO2 and porphyrins absorption. In particular, b-TiO2/29mTHPP presented the highest absorbance intensity than other nanozymes in the range of 400–700 nm due to relatively high intrinsic absorbance of mTHPP (Fig. S12), which was expected to improve the utilization of visible light and thus facilitate the progress of the photosensitization POD-like reaction.

Moreover, the energy band matching could promote the efficient transport of photo-generated carriers at the heterojunction interface, thereby further improving the light-induced POD-like activity of the b-TiO2/porphyrin nanozymes. DFT calculations have been performed the frontier orbitals and energy levels of four porphyrins are given in Fig. S13 and S14, respectively. As can be seen, both the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of four porphyrins concentrate on the porphyrin rings, so the substitution of electron-donating and withdrawing groups (–OH and –COOH) have gradually increased and decreased the HOMO and LUMO energy levels, respectively [41]. Then the bandgap and the conduction band (CB) positions of TPP, pTHPP, pTCPP, and mTHPP were further determined by Tauc plots and Mott–Schottky curves (Fig. S15 and S16), respectively. Moreover, the band structure of b-TiO2 can be obtained by Tauc plots and Ultraviolet Photoelectron Spectroscopy (UPS) in Fig. S17. The schematic diagram of band structures of TPP, pTHPP, pTCPP, mTHPP, and b-TiO2 can be depicted in Fig. 5C. It is not difficult to find that the electron-donating property of the hydroxyl group makes the band structures of pTHPP or mTHPP and b-TiO2 more compatible, thus effectively promoting carrier transport at the heterojunction interface and improving the catalytic activity. On the contrary, the electron-absorbing property of carboxyl group makes the band structures of pTCPP and b-TiO2 mismatch, and pTCPP loses its role as a photosensitizer. To further explore the separation and transfer capability of photoinduced carriers for b-TiO2/29mTHPP, the photo/electrochemical characterizations were investigated. As shown in photoluminescence (PL) spectra (Fig. 5D), b-TiO2/29mTHPP exhibits a lower PL peak intensity than the TiO2/29mTHPP and mTHPP upon excited at 410 nm, which indicate that the sculpture-reduction process and mTHPP modification could efficiently restrain the recombination of the photoinduced carriers. In addition, the fluorescence quantum yield (Table S4) of b-TiO2/29mTHPP is significantly lower than that of mTHPP in line with the PL results. Consistent with POD-like reaction results, the b-TiO2/29mTHPP presents the strongest photocurrent response among that of mTHPP, TiO2, b-TiO2 and TiO2/29mTHPP samples in Fig. 5E. The EIS Nyquist plots (Fig. 5F) under visible light showed that the impedance semicircle radius of b-TiO2/29mTHPP is much smaller than that of mTHPP, TiO2, b-TiO2, and TiO2/29mTHPP samples, indicating the efficient transfer and separation of charge carriers in the b-TiO2/29mTHPP. A combination between high photoinduced carrier separation efficiency and fast charge transfer improves the photosensitized POD-like activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Built-in electric field induced efficient charge transport in b-TiO2/29mTHPP
ce:section.ce:para: Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were employed to investigate the influence of porphyrin substituents on the separation and transmission of charges in nanozymes. As well known, the built-in electric field induced by molecular dipoles plays a crucial role in the effective separation and transport of carriers in porphyrin derivatives [42,43]. The differences in electron-donating/withdrawing ability and position of the substituents at the meso-substituted phenyl groups of porphyrin ring have a significant effect on the molecular dipoles of the respective porphyrin molecules. According to the DFT calculated results, molecular dipoles increased in the order of TPP < pTHPP < pTCPP < mTHPP in Fig. 6 A. The mTHPP with four hydroxyl groups attached at the meta-position of the meso-substituted phenyl groups of porphyrin ring presents the largest diploes of 4.73 Debye among these derivatives, resulting in the largest potential difference between ring center and terminal substituents. Depending on the larger dipole-induced potential difference, the strongest built-in electric field is formed in the mTHPP, which should promote the separation of the photogenerated hole-electron pair [44–46]. Besides, the built-in electric field is positively correlated with the zeta potential according to the reports in the literature [47,48], the zeta potentials of the self-assembled porphyrins increased in the order of TPP < pTHPP < pTCPP < mTHPP (Fig. S18), which further indicated the strongest built-in electric field was formed in the assembled mTHPP and was consisted with the above DFT results.

Furthermore, porphyrins are mostly anchored on the surface of b-TiO2 through hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interaction between substituents and b-TiO2 (Fig. S7), and it is difficult to form effective π-π interactions between porphyrin ring and b-TiO2 [49,50]. Therefore, the direction of the built-in electric field in the porphyrin molecule determines the effective transport of carriers at the heterojunction interface. Because of the high electron affinity of carboxyl group, the direction of the built-in electric field is from the ring center to the carboxyl group for pTCPP in Fig. 6A. For pTHPP or mTHPP, due to the electron-donating nature of the hydroxyl group, the direction of the built-in electric field is from the hydroxyl group to the ring center, as shown in Fig. 6A. Under irradiation, the photogenerated electrons tend to remain in the ring center of pTCPP and transport through the direction of π-π stacking, while holes prefer to migrate outside, which hinders the migration of photo-induced electrons from pTCPP to b-TiO2 (Fig. 6B and S19). The result is exactly the opposite for mTHPP, the photogenerated holes tend to remain in the ring center and the electrons prefer to migrate outside, which promotes the migration of photo-induced electrons from porphyrin to b-TiO2 (Fig. 6B and S19). Therefore, in addition to the largest dipole, mTHPP decorated nanozyme b-TiO2/29mTHPP with the reasonable molecular built-in electric field that promotes the outward migration of photo-generated electrons. This could efficiently enhance the light-induced POD-like activity of the b-TiO2/29mTHPP nanozyme.

In a word, mTHPP is more suitable as a photosensitizer for constructing nanozyme with b-TiO2 owning to two facets: (1) the largest dipoles of mTHPP can induce the formation of a strong and reasonably oriented built-in electric field, which is favorable for the separation and transport of photogenerated carriers. (2) The band structure of mTHPP matches better with b-TiO2, which further promotes the carrier transport from mTHPP to b-TiO2 at the heterojunction interface. All of these could efficiently enhance the light-induced POD-like activity of the b-TiO2/mTHPP nanozyme.

In order to investigate the POD catalytic process, the eleven experiments of POD-like activity were also carried out under the dark. As shown in Fig. 7 A, the systems including b-TiO2 exhibited the same slight absorbance of all systems, which mainly indicates two key points. Firstly, b-TiO2 exhibited observable POD-like activity mainly due to the existence of Fenton-like ion pairs (Ti3+/Ti4+). Secondly, irradiation can significantly increase the POD-like activity, which further illustrated the importance of the separation and transmission of photo-generated carriers. The main active species formed in the POD catalytic process were observed to investigate the catalytic mechanism of b-TiO2/29mTHPP. Four agents including methanol (MA), p-benzoquinone (PBQ), tryptophan (Trp), and disodium edetate (EDTA-2Na) were used as the hydroxyl radical (•OH), superoxide radical (O2 •−), singlet oxygen (1O2), and hole (h+) scavengers [51]. As can be seen in Fig. 7B and C, the absorbance significantly decreased after the addition of methanol, indicating that •OH produced in situ plays a major role in the catalytic system. Meanwhile, no obvious change was observed in the absorbance for the system after adding PBQ and Trp, respectively. To better confirm the generation of •OH radicals, the non-fluorescent terephthalic acid (TA) was used to react with radical •OH to produce 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid with strong fluorescence emission at 430 nm [52]. As shown in Fig. 7D, the fluorescent intensity of the system at 430 nm enhanced along with the increase in the amount of b-TiO2/29mTHPP. This means more •OH radicals have been generated depending on greater the amount of b-TiO2/29mTHPP. This further indicated that b-TiO2/29mTHPP can decompose H2O2 to produce •OH, and thus accelerates the catalytic reaction process, making colorless TMB into blue oxTMB rapidly.

On the basis of the above results, a schematic diagram is proposed to illustrate the catalytic progress of b-TiO2/29mTHPP in Fig. 7E. Photoinduced carriers were effectively separated through the dipole-induced built-in electric field of mTHPP on the surface of b-TiO2 hollow nanospheres. Therefore, the photoinduced electrons will inject into the CB of mTHPP, meanwhile leaving the photoinduced holes in its VB. Moreover, the ultrathin b-TiO2 shell facilitates the multiple scattering of incident light in the internal cavity, which significantly improves the visible light utilization. On this basis, •OH can be generated from the photo-Fenton-like process (Eq. (1)) [31,53] and photoinduced electrons from the CB of mTHPP can cause the reduction of Ti4+ to Ti3+ (Eq (2)), benefitting from the built-in electric field of mTHPP that induces the outward transport of electrons and the good interaction between the b-TiO2 shell and mTHPP, promoted the Fenton-like reaction on b-TiO2. The electrons at the CB of mTHPP can also easily transfer to the CB of b-TiO2 since the energy levels of mTHPP and b-TiO2 are matched well for electron transfer. And through the photocatalytic reduction of adsorbed H2O2 (Eq. (3)) by electrons in the conduction band of the b-TiO2 surface. Finally, the ·OH and h+ with strong oxidizing activity can oxidize the TMB to oxTMB (Eq. (4)). Thus, the mechanism of b-TiO2/29mTHPP for catalytic oxidation of TMB is mainly due to the synergetic effect between mTHPP and b-TiO2 under the visible light to produce active species (•OH and h+).

From the kinetic analysis of b-TiO2/29mTHPP, it can be seen that the POD activity is related to the concentration of H2O2. Consequently, the quantitative determination of H2O2 can be achieved by monitoring the absorbance at 652 nm of TMB. In particular, the ΔAbsorbance (ΔA) increases in a linear manner depending on the concentration of H2O2 ([H2O2]) with the calibration plot (ΔA vs c) of ΔA = 0.641[H2O2] (mM) + 0.0869 (R2 = 0.998) with a low of detection of 0.001 mM, in Fig. S20A and S20B. As we know, cholesterol in the sample is first oxidized by ChOx to produce H2O2, which can be subsequently used for the catalytic oxidation of TMB. Under the typical assay conditions, two kinds of correlation were also observed between the ΔAbsorbance (ΔA) and the concentration of cholesterol ([Cho]), ΔA = 0.376[Cho] + 0.0879 (R2 = 0.991) in the range of 0.005–1.45 mM and ΔA = 0.019[Cho] + 0.606 (R2 = 0.924) in the range of 1.45–10 mM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 1.2 μM (3 × SB/slope) in Fig. 8 A and B. It is worth noting that, the present performance in terms of the low LOD (1.2 μM), wide range (0.005–10 mM) and high sensitivity is among the best result reported so far for the metal oxide-based sensor to cholesterol in Table S5. Moreover, the b-TiO2/29mTHPP-based colorimetric method has the advantages of easy miniaturization, simple operation, and low power consumption compared to other methods.

The selective experiments were performed to verify the selectivity of b-TiO2/29mTHPP biosensor towards H2O2 and cholesterol, including uric acid, ascorbic acid, L-tyrosine, D-serine, D-histidine, L-Arginine, lactose, glucose, fructose, D-cysteine, Ca2+, Mg2+, K+, and Na+. As shown in Fig. 8C, 8D, and S20C, S20D, although the concentration of interferences is 10 times that of H2O2 and cholesterol, the response of b-TiO2/29mTHPP to interferents is negligible, and the detection of H2O2 and cholesterol by b-TiO2/29mTHPP has no significant effect in the presence of interferent. It is suggested the excellent selectivity of the proposed method for determination of H2O2 and cholesterol.

Robustness is very important for the practical application of the catalyst, such as temperature, pH, reusability, and long-term stability. According to the literature, the activity of HRP was significantly reduced after treating at over 40 °C or pH < 5 for 2 h [54]. To evaluate the robustness of b-TiO2/29mTHPP, stability experiments were performed after incubation of the catalysts in different temperatures and pH for 3 h. As shown in Fig. S21A and S21B, the catalytic activity of b-TiO2/29mTHPP was maintained at a relatively high level over a broad pH range of 1–12 and temperature range of 0–80 °C. The reusability of b-TiO2/29mTHPP was investigated by cycle experiments in Fig. S21C. Although the relative activity of b-TiO2/29mTHPP decreased with the increasing number of reuses, it maintained higher than 90% after 30 cycles. As presented in Fig. S21D, the obtained b-TiO2/29mTHPP kept an acceptable relative activity even after 180 days. All of these results confirmed the excellent robustness of the b-TiO2/29mTHPP.

The total cholesterol in human serum includes free cholesterol and cholesterol ester, among which cholesterol ester can be decomposed into free cholesterol by cholesterol esterase. Consequently, the simultaneous introduction of cholesterol oxidase and cholesterol esterase can achieve the detection of total cholesterol in human serum. The comparison between the test results of the proposed method and the hospital clinical test results is shown in Table 1 . It is not difficult to see that the test results are basically consistent with the clinical results. The prepared b-TiO2/29mTHPP biosensor shows the advantages of fast detection efficiency, high stability, and low cost when compared with conventional methods, and demonstrates broad application prospects in clinical diagnosis.

In conclusion, DFT results indicate that the strong built-in electric field induced by the large dipoles of mTHPP effectively improves the separation efficiency of carriers and promotes the transmission of photo-generated carriers between mTHPP and b-TiO2. Therefore, the prepared b-TiO2/29mTHPP with excellent POD-like activity under visible light achieved rapid oxidation of the chromogenic substrate TMB in a few seconds. By means of POD-like activity of b-TiO2/29mTHPP combined with the Chox, a colorimetric sensing platform has been constructed for the quantitative determination of cholesterol in 0.005–10 mM with high selectivity and low detection limit. The excellent activity of b-TiO2/29mTHPP is attributed to the effective separation of photocarriers which are adjusted by the built-in electric field of mTHPP. And, the built-in electric field formed in mTHPP is conducive to the rapid multi-channel transmission of photogenerated electrons from mTHPP to b-TiO2. It indicates that changing the intensity and direction of the built-in electric field for conjugated dyes by substituents is an effective strategy to enhance the light-induced POD-like activity。
